entries.definition: A phrase headed by an adjective that typically functions as a modifier within NPs or as a predicative complement in VPs.
entries.excerpt: A phrase headed by an adjective that typically functions as a modifier within NPs or as a predicative complement in VPs.

related_chapters.title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar

entries.plain_hint: A phrase headed by an adjective that typically functions as a modifier within NPs or as a predicative complement in VPs.

entries.definition: A phrase headed by an adverb. Functions primarily as a modifier of verbs, adjectives, other adverbs, clauses, and PPs. Rarely functions as a complement. Accepts degree modifiers like very, too, so but not right, straight, just (in Standard English).
entries.excerpt: A phrase headed by an adverb. Functions primarily as a modifier of verbs, adjectives, other adverbs, clauses, and PPs. Rarely functions as a complement. Accepts degree modifiers l…
entries.plain_hint: A phrase headed by an adverb.

entries.definition: Negation expressed through prefixes (like un-, dis-, non-) or suffixes attached to words rather than through separate negative words.
entries.excerpt: Negation expressed through prefixes (like un-, dis-, non-) or suffixes attached to words rather than through separate negative words.
entries.plain_hint: Negation expressed through prefixes (like un-, dis-, non-) or suffixes attached to words rather than through separate negative words.

entries.definition: The matching of verb form to properties of the subject, particularly in present tense with third-person singular subjects.
entries.excerpt: The matching of verb form to properties of the subject, particularly in present tense with third-person singular subjects.

related_chapters.title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar

entries.plain_hint: The matching of verb form to properties of the subject, particularly in present tense with third-person singular subjects.

entries.definition: A variant of a phoneme that doesn't change meaning in a given language. For example, the aspirated [tʰ] in top and unaspirated [t] in stop are allophones of /t/ in English.
entries.excerpt: A variant of a phoneme that doesn't change meaning in a given language. For example, the aspirated [tʰ] in top and unaspirated [t] in stop are allophones of /t/ in English.
entries.plain_hint: A variant of a phoneme that doesn't change meaning in a given language.

entries.definition: A grammatical system that typically denotes how a situation extends over time, including simple, progressive, perfect, and perfect-progressive combinations.
entries.excerpt: A grammatical system that typically denotes how a situation extends over time, including simple, progressive, perfect, and perfect-progressive combinations.
entries.plain_hint: A grammatical system that typically denotes how a situation extends over time, including simple, progressive, perfect, and perfect-progressive combinations.

entries.definition: A process in connected speech where a sound becomes more like a neighbouring sound. For instance, ten books may be pronounced as [tem bks].
entries.excerpt: A process in connected speech where a sound becomes more like a neighbouring sound. For instance, ten books may be pronounced as [tem bks].
entries.plain_hint: A process in connected speech where a sound becomes more like a neighbouring sound.

entries.definition: A modifier that appears within an NP, typically before the head N, and attributes some property to it.
entries.excerpt: A modifier that appears within an NP, typically before the head N, and attributes some property to it.
entries.plain_hint: A modifier that appears within an NP, typically before the head N, and attributes some property to it.

entries.definition: A verb that carries tense, aspect, or modality (e.g., be, have, do, can, will) and has the NICER properties: Negation, Inversion, Contraction, Ellipsis, and Rebuttal.
entries.excerpt: A verb that carries tense, aspect, or modality (e.g., be, have, do, can, will) and has the NICER properties: Negation, Inversion, Contraction, Ellipsis, and Rebuttal.

entries.plain_hint: A verb that carries tense, aspect, or modality (e.g., be, have, do, can, will) and has the NICER properties: Negation, Inversion, Contraction, Ellipsis, and Rebuttal.

entries.definition: Negation that applies to an entire clause, subdivided into verbal negation (using not with verbs) and non-verbal negation (using not with other constituents).
entries.excerpt: Negation that applies to an entire clause, subdivided into verbal negation (using not with verbs) and non-verbal negation (using not with other constituents).
entries.plain_hint: Negation that applies to an entire clause, subdivided into verbal negation (using not with verbs) and non-verbal negation (using not with other constituents).

entries.definition: A grammatical structure headed by a verb phrase and usually containing a subject.
entries.excerpt: A grammatical structure headed by a verb phrase and usually containing a subject.
entries.plain_hint: A grammatical structure headed by a verb phrase and usually containing a subject.

entries.definition: The consonant(s) following the (vowel) nucleus in a syllable. In cat /kaet/, /t/ is the coda.
entries.excerpt: The consonant(s) following the (vowel) nucleus in a syllable. In cat /kaet/, /t/ is the coda.
entries.plain_hint: The consonant(s) following the (vowel) nucleus in a syllable.

entries.definition: Alternating between languages or language varieties within a single conversation, typically reflecting changes in context or social dynamics.
entries.excerpt: Alternating between languages or language varieties within a single conversation, typically reflecting changes in context or social dynamics.

related_chapters.title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach

entries.plain_hint: Alternating between languages or language varieties within a single conversation, typically reflecting changes in context or social dynamics.

entries.definition: The more-or-less arbitrary requirement that certain words license specific prepositions as complements (e.g., depend on, interested in, proud of). A major challenge for language learners.
entries.excerpt: The more-or-less arbitrary requirement that certain words license specific prepositions as complements (e.g., depend on, interested in, proud of). A major challenge for language l…
entries.plain_hint: The more-or-less arbitrary requirement that certain words license specific prepositions as complements (e.g., depend on, interested in, proud of).

entries.definition: The systematic way individual words in head function control what can complete the phrase (e.g., an NP, a PP, a clause, etc.), with different words selecting different types of complements.
entries.excerpt: The systematic way individual words in head function control what can complete the phrase (e.g., an NP, a PP, a clause, etc.), with different words selecting different types of co…
entries.plain_hint: The systematic way individual words in head function control what can complete the phrase (e.g., an NP, a PP, a clause, etc.), with different words selecting different types of complements.

entries.definition: The continuous flow of sounds in natural speech, often involving processes that modify individual word pronunciations.
entries.excerpt: The continuous flow of sounds in natural speech, often involving processes that modify individual word pronunciations.
entries.plain_hint: The continuous flow of sounds in natural speech, often involving processes that modify individual word pronunciations.

entries.definition: A conventional pairing of form and meaning that exists at various levels of linguistic structure, from specific phrases to general syntactic patterns.
entries.excerpt: A conventional pairing of form and meaning that exists at various levels of linguistic structure, from specific phrases to general syntactic patterns.
entries.plain_hint: A conventional pairing of form and meaning that exists at various levels of linguistic structure, from specific phrases to general syntactic patterns.

entries.definition: Diagnostic based on the principle that constituents tend to coordinate with like constituents: PPs coordinate with PPs, AdvPs with AdvPs.
entries.excerpt: Diagnostic based on the principle that constituents tend to coordinate with like constituents: PPs coordinate with PPs, AdvPs with AdvPs.
entries.plain_hint: Diagnostic based on the principle that constituents tend to coordinate with like constituents: PPs coordinate with PPs, AdvPs with AdvPs.

entries.definition: An example that disproves a proposed rule or definition, being a case where the rule fails. Good for testing linguistic definitions.
entries.excerpt: An example that disproves a proposed rule or definition, being a case where the rule fails. Good for testing linguistic definitions.

entries.plain_hint: An example that disproves a proposed rule or definition, being a case where the rule fails.

entries.definition: A verb lacking one or more of the typical verb forms (e.g., must, which has no past tense).
entries.excerpt: A verb lacking one or more of the typical verb forms (e.g., must, which has no past tense).

related_chapters.title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar

entries.plain_hint: A verb lacking one or more of the typical verb forms (e.g., must, which has no past tense).

entries.definition: A grammatical property that indicates whether a noun phrase refers to something specific and identifiable to both speaker and hearer, typically marked by a determiner.
entries.excerpt: A grammatical property that indicates whether a noun phrase refers to something specific and identifiable to both speaker and hearer, typically marked by a determiner.
entries.plain_hint: A grammatical property that indicates whether a noun phrase refers to something specific and identifiable to both speaker and hearer, typically marked by a determiner.

entries.definition: A phrase that depends on the head. For example, the is a dependent in the cake.
entries.excerpt: A phrase that depends on the head. For example, the is a dependent in the cake.
entries.plain_hint: A phrase that depends on the head.

entries.definition: A word of the lexical category that typically specifies the reference and number of noun phrases, including articles (the, a), demonstratives (this, that), and quantifiers (some, many), distinguished from adjectives by their function and distribution.
entries.excerpt: A word of the lexical category that typically specifies the reference and number of noun phrases, including articles (the, a), demonstratives (this, that), and quantifiers (some,…
entries.plain_hint: A word of the lexical category that typically specifies the reference and number of noun phrases, including articles (the, a), demonstratives (this, that), and quantifiers (some, many), distinguished from adjectives by their function and distribution.

entries.definition: A phrase headed by a determinative that mostly functions as the determiner in an NP, such as most, not many, almost every, and just about all.
entries.excerpt: A phrase headed by a determinative that mostly functions as the determiner in an NP, such as most, not many, almost every, and just about all.
entries.plain_hint: A phrase headed by a determinative that mostly functions as the determiner in an NP, such as most, not many, almost every, and just about all.

entries.definition: A dependent in a noun phrase that marks it as definite or indefinite (e.g., a pen, the pen, much more air, almost ten pens, my pen).
entries.excerpt: A dependent in a noun phrase that marks it as definite or indefinite (e.g., a pen, the pen, much more air, almost ten pens, my pen).
entries.plain_hint: A dependent in a noun phrase that marks it as definite or indefinite (e.g., a pen, the pen, much more air, almost ten pens, my pen).

entries.definition: A constituent selected by a transitive verb, usually able to be identified through passivization tests (becomes the subject in passive constructions).
entries.excerpt: A constituent selected by a transitive verb, usually able to be identified through passivization tests (becomes the subject in passive constructions).
entries.plain_hint: A constituent selected by a transitive verb, usually able to be identified through passivization tests (becomes the subject in passive constructions).

entries.definition: An environment where inferences flow from general to specific (if not all students is true, then not some students is also true), typically licensing negative polarity items.
entries.excerpt: An environment where inferences flow from general to specific (if not all students is true, then not some students is also true), typically licensing negative polarity items.
entries.plain_hint: An environment where inferences flow from general to specific (if not all students is true, then not some students is also true), typically licensing negative polarity items.

entries.definition: The mistaken belief that words have (or should have) only one "true" meaning.
entries.excerpt: The mistaken belief that words have (or should have) only one "true" meaning.

entries.plain_hint: The mistaken belief that words have (or should have) only one "true" meaning.

entries.definition: The view that prepositions can take various types of complements (NPs, clauses, PPs) or no complement at all, paralleling how verbs take different complements. Contrasts with traditional view requiring NP complements.
entries.excerpt: The view that prepositions can take various types of complements (NPs, clauses, PPs) or no complement at all, paralleling how verbs take different complements. Contrasts with trad…
entries.plain_hint: The view that prepositions can take various types of complements (NPs, clauses, PPs) or no complement at all, paralleling how verbs take different complements.

entries.definition: The connection between how something is said (its linguistic form) and what it means in a particular context. Grammaticality often depends on using established form-meaning pairs within a speech community.
entries.excerpt: The connection between how something is said (its linguistic form) and what it means in a particular context. Grammaticality often depends on using established form-meaning pairs…
entries.plain_hint: The connection between how something is said (its linguistic form) and what it means in a particular context.

entries.definition: The phenomenon whereby words from different lexical categories can perform similar syntactic functions.
entries.excerpt: The phenomenon whereby words from different lexical categories can perform similar syntactic functions.
entries.plain_hint: The phenomenon whereby words from different lexical categories can perform similar syntactic functions.

entries.definition: The property of adjectives, adverbs, and some determinatives and prepositions that allows them to be modified by degree words like very, quite, or extremely, and to form comparative and superlative forms.
entries.excerpt: The property of adjectives, adverbs, and some determinatives and prepositions that allows them to be modified by degree words like very, quite, or extremely, and to form comparati…
entries.plain_hint: The property of adjectives, adverbs, and some determinatives and prepositions that allows them to be modified by degree words like very, quite, or extremely, and to form comparative and superlative forms.

entries.definition: The degree to which a construction conforms to the accepted form-meaning pairings of a specific language community, reflecting regularities in syntax, morphology, and socio-cognitive norms, and regardless of how unconventional the meaning is.
entries.excerpt: The degree to which a construction conforms to the accepted form-meaning pairings of a specific language community, reflecting regularities in syntax, morphology, and socio-cognit…
entries.plain_hint: The degree to which a construction conforms to the accepted form-meaning pairings of a specific language community, reflecting regularities in syntax, morphology, and socio-cognitive norms, and regardless of how unconventional the meaning is.

entries.definition: The word in a phrase that determines the phrase's grammatical type. Cake is the head of the NP the chocolate cake; tall is the head of the AdjP very tall.
entries.excerpt: The word in a phrase that determines the phrase's grammatical type. Cake is the head of the NP the chocolate cake; tall is the head of the AdjP very tall.

entries.definition: A constituent that precedes the direct object and typically represents the recipient or beneficiary of an action, often paraphraseable with to or for phrases.
entries.excerpt: A constituent that precedes the direct object and typically represents the recipient or beneficiary of an action, often paraphraseable with to or for phrases.
entries.plain_hint: A constituent that precedes the direct object and typically represents the recipient or beneficiary of an action, often paraphraseable with to or for phrases.

entries.definition: The rise and fall of voice pitch over phrases and sentences, conveying various linguistic and paralinguistic meanings.
entries.excerpt: The rise and fall of voice pitch over phrases and sentences, conveying various linguistic and paralinguistic meanings.
entries.plain_hint: The rise and fall of voice pitch over phrases and sentences, conveying various linguistic and paralinguistic meanings.

entries.definition: A historical process describing how negation markers evolve and change in languages, named after Otto Jespersen. English evolved from Old English ne through stages of reinforcement to modern contracted forms like don't.
entries.excerpt: A historical process describing how negation markers evolve and change in languages, named after Otto Jespersen. English evolved from Old English ne through stages of reinforcemen…
entries.plain_hint: A historical process describing how negation markers evolve and change in languages, named after Otto Jespersen.

entries.definition: A category of words (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) having a coherent set of properties.
entries.excerpt: A category of words (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) having a coherent set of properties.
entries.plain_hint: A category of words (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) having a coherent set of properties.

entries.definition: All non-auxiliary verbs. Lexical verbs typically carry the primary semantic content, contrasting with auxiliary verbs that provide grammatical information.
entries.excerpt: All non-auxiliary verbs. Lexical verbs typically carry the primary semantic content, contrasting with auxiliary verbs that provide grammatical information.

entries.plain_hint: All non-auxiliary verbs.

entries.definition: A multi-word predicate where a semantically light verb combines with a noun to express an action (e.g., take a walk vs. walk).

entries.definition: The connection of sounds across word boundaries in connected speech, as in an apple.
entries.excerpt: The connection of sounds across word boundaries in connected speech, as in an apple.
entries.plain_hint: The connection of sounds across word boundaries in connected speech, as in an apple.

entries.definition: The principle that a grammatical category can perform multiple functions and a single function can be performed by multiple categories.
entries.excerpt: The principle that a grammatical category can perform multiple functions and a single function can be performed by multiple categories.

entries.definition: A type of auxiliary verb expressing meanings like possibility, necessity, or future time (e.g., can, must, will).

entries.definition: The expression of meanings such as possibility, necessity, permission, and obligation.
entries.excerpt: The expression of meanings such as possibility, necessity, permission, and obligation.
entries.plain_hint: The expression of meanings such as possibility, necessity, permission, and obligation.

entries.definition: Diagnostic for distinguishing PPs from AdvPs: PPs generally accept right, straight, just as modifiers; AdvPs generally accept very, too, so.
entries.excerpt: Diagnostic for distinguishing PPs from AdvPs: PPs generally accept right, straight, just as modifiers; AdvPs generally accept very, too, so.
entries.plain_hint: Diagnostic for distinguishing PPs from AdvPs: PPs generally accept right, straight, just as modifiers; AdvPs generally accept very, too, so.

entries.definition: One that must be present for membership in a category. For example, having a head is a necessary condition for being a human, and having a head is a necessary condition for being a phrase.

entries.definition: A word or phrase (like any, ever, yet, at all) that typically appears only in negative contexts or other downward-entailing environments.
entries.excerpt: A word or phrase (like any, ever, yet, at all) that typically appears only in negative contexts or other downward-entailing environments.

entries.definition: The distinctive properties of auxiliary verbs: Negation, Inversion, Contraction, Ellipsis, and Rebuttal.

entries.definition: Alternative term for negative polarity item, emphasizing that these items appear in various non-positive contexts, not just overtly negative ones.

entries.definition: Claims about how language ought to be used, rather than descriptions of how it is used. Related to prescriptive grammar but broader in scope.
entries.excerpt: Claims about how language ought to be used, rather than descriptions of how it is used. Related to prescriptive grammar but broader in scope.

entries.definition: A word that can head a noun phrase. Nouns can typically be singular or plural, follow determiners like the and a, and take AdjP modifiers. They can denote almost anything (entities, actions, states, concepts), which is why denotation alone can't define them.

entries.definition: A count noun allows counting and plural marking (1 book, 2 ideas); a non-count noun doesn't (*1 furniture, *2 groceries). The distinction involves both a semantic construal (whether the referent is seen as individuated) and a grammatical marking (whether the noun takes plural and numeral dependents).

entries.definition: The traditional (but problematic) view that defines prepositions as words that must take NP complements. Creates unnecessary complexity by forcing words like before into three different categories.

entries.definition: The core of a syllable, typically a vowel. In cat /kaet/, // is the nucleus.
entries.excerpt: The core of a syllable, typically a vowel. In cat /kaet/, // is the nucleus.

entries.definition: The consonant(s) preceding the nucleus in a syllable. In cat /kaet/, /k/ is the onset.
entries.excerpt: The consonant(s) preceding the nucleus in a syllable. In cat /kaet/, /k/ is the onset.
entries.plain_hint: The consonant(s) preceding the nucleus in a syllable.

entries.definition: A non-tensed verb form, either present participle (-ing form) or past participle (typically -ed or irregular forms), used in various constructions including progressive and perfect aspects.
entries.excerpt: A non-tensed verb form, either present participle (-ing form) or past participle (typically -ed or irregular forms), used in various constructions including progressive and perfec…

entries.definition: A preposition functioning as part of a phrasal verb that can appear either before or after an NP object (pick up the pen/pick the pen up) but must follow pronoun objects (pick it up, not *pick up it).

entries.definition: An aspectual form using have + past participle that relates a situation to a later reference point, often expressing some relevance to that reference point.
entries.excerpt: An aspectual form using have + past participle that relates a situation to a later reference point, often expressing some relevance to that reference point.

entries.definition: The smallest unit of sound that can distinguish meaning in a language. Changing /b/ to /p/ turns bat into pat.
entries.excerpt: The smallest unit of sound that can distinguish meaning in a language. Changing /b/ to /p/ turns bat into pat.
entries.plain_hint: The smallest unit of sound that can distinguish meaning in a language.

entries.definition: The rules governing possible sound combinations in a language. English allows /sl/ as an onset (e.g., slide) but not as a coda.
entries.excerpt: The rules governing possible sound combinations in a language. English allows /sl/ as an onset (e.g., slide) but not as a coda.
entries.plain_hint: The rules governing possible sound combinations in a language.

entries.definition: A syntactic unit, such as noun phrases (NPs), verb phrases (VPs), or adjective phrases (AdjPs), consisting of a head and any dependents.
entries.excerpt: A syntactic unit, such as noun phrases (NPs), verb phrases (VPs), or adjective phrases (AdjPs), consisting of a head and any dependents.
entries.plain_hint: A syntactic unit, such as noun phrases (NPs), verb phrases (VPs), or adjective phrases (AdjPs), consisting of a head and any dependents.

entries.definition: A verb + preposition(s) combination that often expresses idiomatic meaning (e.g., give up, look up, take off). The preposition may function as a particle that can move around NP objects but not pronoun objects.

entries.plain_hint: A verb + preposition(s) combination that often expresses idiomatic meaning (e.g., give up, look up, take off).

entries.definition: A grammatical structure consisting of a head word and any optional dependents. The head determines the phrase's type: an NP is headed by a noun, a VP by a verb. A phrase can be a single word (Stop! is a VP).

entries.definition: Denoting more than one, or a number that doesn't fit singular (e.g., zero, fractions).

entries.definition: The phenomenon whereby certain words and phrases are restricted to appearing in either positive or negative linguistic contexts.
entries.excerpt: The phenomenon whereby certain words and phrases are restricted to appearing in either positive or negative linguistic contexts.
entries.plain_hint: The phenomenon whereby certain words and phrases are restricted to appearing in either positive or negative linguistic contexts.

entries.definition: A word or phrase (like some, already, a fair chance) that resists appearing in negative contexts and prefers positive or affirmative environments.
entries.excerpt: A word or phrase (like some, already, a fair chance) that resists appearing in negative contexts and prefers positive or affirmative environments.
entries.plain_hint: A word or phrase (like some, already, a fair chance) that resists appearing in negative contexts and prefers positive or affirmative environments.

entries.definition: Negation indicated by context, tone, or implicature rather than through explicit negative markers in the sentence structure.
entries.excerpt: Negation indicated by context, tone, or implicature rather than through explicit negative markers in the sentence structure.
entries.plain_hint: Negation indicated by context, tone, or implicature rather than through explicit negative markers in the sentence structure.

entries.definition: A phrase that follows verbs like be, seem, become, remain, look, and feel and that describes or identifies the subject (or, with some verbs, the object).
entries.excerpt: A phrase that follows verbs like be, seem, become, remain, look, and feel and that describes or identifies the subject (or, with some verbs, the object).
entries.plain_hint: A phrase that follows verbs like be, seem, become, remain, look, and feel and that describes or identifies the subject (or, with some verbs, the object).

entries.definition: Diagnostic showing that PPs (but not AdvPs) can function as complement after verbs that license predicative complements: The meeting is at noon but not *The meeting is quickly.
entries.excerpt: Diagnostic showing that PPs (but not AdvPs) can function as complement after verbs that license predicative complements: The meeting is at noon but not *The meeting is quickly.
entries.plain_hint: Diagnostic showing that PPs (but not AdvPs) can function as complement after verbs that license predicative complements: The meeting is at noon but not *The meeting is quickly.

entries.definition: Fronting an entire PP in an interrogative or relative, typically in formal contexts: About what did you disagree? versus the more common stranded version What did you disagree about?

entries.definition: A phrase headed by a preposition. Functions as both modifier and complement in various phrase types. Can modify nouns (unlike AdvPs) and frequently serves as predicative complement after be, seem, remain.
entries.excerpt: A phrase headed by a preposition. Functions as both modifier and complement in various phrase types. Can modify nouns (unlike AdvPs) and frequently serves as predicative complemen…
entries.plain_hint: A phrase headed by a preposition.

entries.definition: When a preposition appears without its complement because the complement has been fronted, as in questions (What are you looking at?) or relative clauses (the person you spoke to).

entries.definition: An aspectual form using be + -ing participle that expresses relevance not just to what continues but to its eventual conclusion.
entries.excerpt: An aspectual form using be + -ing participle that expresses relevance not just to what continues but to its eventual conclusion.

entries.definition: The weakening or omission of sounds in connected speech, often in unstressed syllables. For example, going to may be pronounced as gonna.
entries.excerpt: The weakening or omission of sounds in connected speech, often in unstressed syllables. For example, going to may be pronounced as gonna.
entries.plain_hint: The weakening or omission of sounds in connected speech, often in unstressed syllables.

entries.definition: A syntactic pattern where a verb is followed by a result phrase describing the outcome of the situation (e.g., hammer the metal flat).
entries.excerpt: A syntactic pattern where a verb is followed by a result phrase describing the outcome of the situation (e.g., hammer the metal flat).

entries.definition: The timing pattern of stressed and unstressed syllables in speech.

entries.definition: The portion of a sentence that falls under the semantic influence of a negative element, determining what is being negated and affecting the interpretation of modal verbs and other elements.

entries.definition: The historical process by which words gradually lose their original concrete meaning and become more grammatical or functional, often occurring in grammaticalization.
entries.excerpt: The historical process by which words gradually lose their original concrete meaning and become more grammatical or functional, often occurring in grammaticalization.
entries.plain_hint: The historical process by which words gradually lose their original concrete meaning and become more grammatical or functional, often occurring in grammaticalization.

entries.definition: A sequence of verbs that together express a single complex event, typically involving motion and purpose or manner.
entries.excerpt: A sequence of verbs that together express a single complex event, typically involving motion and purpose or manner.

entries.definition: A linguistic feature that distinguishes members of one social group from another, often used (consciously or unconsciously) as a test of belonging. From Hebrew sibbolet.

entries.definition: A type of fricative or affricate consonant characterized by a high-pitched hissing sound, produced when air is forced through a narrow channel between the tongue and the roof of the mouth or teeth.

entries.definition: The most widely accepted variety of a language, particularly in formal, educational, and professional contexts. Not inherently superior to other varieties, but backed by social and institutional power.

entries.definition: The relative prominence given to certain syllables or words, through a combination of pitch, length, and loudness.
entries.excerpt: The relative prominence given to certain syllables or words, through a combination of pitch, length, and loudness.

entries.definition: Negation that applies to constituents smaller than a full clause, such as negated noun phrases or prepositional phrases.

entries.definition: The grammatical function typically performed by the first major constituent in a sentence, controlling verb agreement and identified through tests like tag questions and passivization.

entries.definition: One that, if present, guarantees membership in a category. For example, being a square is a sufficient condition for being a rectangle, and being an NP is a sufficient condition for being a phrase.

entries.definition: Features of speech that extend over more than one sound segment, including stress, intonation, and rhythm.

entries.definition: A unit of pronunciation typically consisting of a vowel (nucleus) with optional consonants before (onset) and after (coda).

entries.definition: The relational grammatical role that a word or phrase plays in sentence structure, including head, subject, object, modifier, complement, and determiner functions.

entries.definition: A grammatical system that typically denotes the time of the situation; English has two tenses (past and present).
entries.excerpt: A grammatical system that typically denotes the time of the situation; English has two tenses (past and present).

entries.definition: A word that can head a verb phrase. Most verbs have a past-tense form, an -s form, an -ing form, and a plain form. They typically denote actions or states, but this is a tendency, not a definition; existence is a noun and resemble is a verb.

entries.definition: A phrase headed by a verb, potentially including modifiers and complements. Can be modified by adverb phrases but not adjective phrases.

entries.definition: The vibration of the vocal cords during the production of a speech sound. Voiced sounds (like /b/, /d/, /g/) involve this vibration, while voiceless sounds (like /p/, /t/, /k/) do not.
